stories.headline: U.S. and Israel Strike Iranian University, Residential Areas; IRGC Intelligence Chief Killed as Conflict Escalates
stories.summary:
U.S. and Israeli forces carried out sweeping strikes across at least a dozen Iranian cities on Monday, killing at least 34 people including six children, according to reports from Iranian state media and Al Jazeera. Among the targets was Sharif University in Tehran, one of Iran's premier scientific institutions, which sustained severe damage to its mosque and laboratories. Iranian officials said at least 30 universities have been struck since the conflict began on February 28.

Major-General Majid Khademi, head of the Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps Intelligence Organisation, was confirmed killed in what the IRGC described as a "terrorist attack by the American-Zionist enemy." Israel also claimed to have killed Asghar Bakeri, the leader of the IRGC's undercover unit within the Quds Force, alleging he had planned attacks on Israeli and U.S. targets across multiple countries.

Iranian Vice President Mohammad Reza Aref condemned the strike on Sharif University, calling it a "bunker-buster bomb attack" and a "symbol of Trump's madness and ignorance." He argued that Iran's scientific knowledge could not be destroyed by bombs, adding that "the true fortress is the will of our professors and elites." The International Federation of Red Cross and Red Crescent Societies expressed deep concern over reports that another Iranian Red Crescent ambulance had been struck.

The strikes follow President Trump's threat to target Iranian power plants and bridges by Tuesday evening if Tehran does not reopen the Strait of Hormuz. Iran has maintained the strait will remain blocked until it receives compensation for war damages. Diplomatic efforts continue behind the scenes, with Oman, Pakistan, and Egypt working to keep communications open between Washington and Tehran.

stories.headline: March Jobs Report Shows Strong Rebound; Oil Shock Rattles Financial Markets
stories.summary:
The U.S. Labor Department reported Friday that hiring rebounded sharply in March, reversing a loss of 133,000 jobs in February. The job gains came in at roughly three times what economists had forecast, offering a positive signal for the broader labor market amid ongoing geopolitical turbulence.

Despite the encouraging employment data, a significant shock to global oil supplies linked to the Iran conflict is rattling financial markets, driving up prices at the pump and raising fears of a more substantial economic hit. Analysts warn that sustained disruption to shipping through the Strait of Hormuz could amplify inflationary pressures already weighing on consumers and businesses.

Governments' borrowing costs are being pulled in opposite directions as markets weigh the strong labor data against the risk of prolonged conflict and energy disruption. European nations are also facing friction with the Trump administration over their reluctance to support U.S. military operations against Iran, adding a diplomatic dimension to the economic uncertainty.

The jobs report provides the Federal Reserve with a complicated backdrop as it weighs monetary policy decisions. A resilient labor market could reduce pressure to cut interest rates, while an oil-driven inflation spike may complicate any move to ease borrowing costs in the months ahead.

stories.headline: Israel Strikes Iran's South Pars Gas Field as Iran-U.S. War Intensifies
stories.summary:
Israeli Defense Minister Israel Katz announced Monday that Israel has attacked Iran's South Pars gas field, one of the world's largest natural gas reserves, marking a significant escalation in the ongoing conflict. The strike represents a major expansion of the targeting strategy, moving beyond military infrastructure to hit Iran's critical energy assets. South Pars, located in the Persian Gulf, is central to Iran's economy and global energy exports.

The strikes came amid a broader wave of Israeli and U.S. military action targeting sites across Iran, including a residential building in Baharestan County southwest of Tehran where at least 13 people were killed, according to Iranian state media. Israel's military confirmed it conducted a wave of strikes on targets in Tehran, though it did not immediately provide details on all targets. Separately, Iranian missile strikes on the Israeli city of Haifa killed at least two people and trapped others under rubble.

The attack on South Pars carries profound economic and geopolitical implications. Iran relies heavily on the gas field for domestic energy supply and export revenue, and any sustained damage could further destabilize an economy already under severe pressure from the conflict. Global energy markets, already rattled by the closure of the Strait of Hormuz, are likely to react sharply to news of strikes on major production infrastructure.

The escalation comes as diplomatic efforts continue in the background, with Oman, Pakistan, and Egypt all reportedly working to maintain communication channels between Washington and Tehran. A ceasefire proposal has been presented to both sides, though Iran has publicly rejected Trump's ultimatum demanding the Strait of Hormuz be reopened by Tuesday at 8 p.m. Eastern time. Iranian officials have insisted the strait will remain blocked until Iran receives compensation for war damages.

stories.headline: South Korea's Intelligence Agency Says Kim Jong Un's Daughter Is Being Groomed as His Successor
stories.summary:
South Korea's National Intelligence Service has told lawmakers it now holds credible intelligence indicating that North Korean leader Kim Jong Un's teenage daughter, Kim Ju Ae, has been positioned as his designated successor, South Korean legislators said Monday. The assessment was prompted in part by a recent public appearance in which Kim Ju Ae was shown driving a tank, an event analysts believe was deliberately staged to project her authority and military credentials to North Korea's armed forces and political elite.

Kim Ju Ae has appeared publicly alongside her father at a growing number of high-profile military and political events in recent years, fueling speculation about her role in the country's succession planning. South Korea's spy agency previously assessed that her prominence was largely ceremonial, but the new intelligence represents a shift toward viewing her position as substantive. Analysts note that Kim Jong Un himself was prepared for succession through a similar process of gradual public exposure over many years.

The development carries significant implications for regional security and long-term North Korean policy. A dynastic succession to a teenager would be unprecedented in modern North Korean history and could introduce a period of internal uncertainty, particularly within the military and ruling party. Foreign governments and intelligence agencies are likely to intensify monitoring of the leadership transition.

North Korea has not commented on the South Korean intelligence assessment. Pyongyang routinely dismisses South Korean and Western characterizations of its internal affairs as fabrications. Independent verification of succession arrangements in the hermetically sealed state remains extremely difficult.

stories.headline: U.S. Strikes Iran's Largest Bridge as Trump Sets Tuesday Deadline Over Strait of Hormuz
stories.summary:
U.S. forces struck Iran's largest bridge as President Donald Trump set a new deadline of Tuesday at 8:00 p.m. Eastern Time for Iran to reopen the Strait of Hormuz, warning that power plants and additional infrastructure would be targeted if Tehran does not comply. Trump issued the threat in an expletive-laden social media post on Easter Sunday, while the International Atomic Energy Agency confirmed that recent military strikes came close to Iran's Bushehr nuclear power plant, though the facility itself was not damaged.

Iran's senior officials responded defiantly, stating the strait would remain blocked until Iran receives compensation for war damages. Meanwhile, Israeli forces carried out a wave of strikes on residential areas in Tehran, killing at least 13 people in Baharestan County, while Iranian missile strikes on the Israeli city of Haifa killed two people, with rescuers searching for additional missing individuals in the rubble of a struck residential building.

Behind the scenes, diplomatic efforts continued as Oman held last-ditch talks with Iran over the Strait of Hormuz, with Pakistan and Egypt also working to keep communications open between Washington and Tehran. A ceasefire proposal circulated to both sides was reported to potentially take effect on Monday, though no agreement had been announced.

The escalating conflict has drawn sharp concern from allies and adversaries alike. Supporters of Trump's posture argue maximum pressure is the only language Iran's leadership understands, while critics warn that targeting civilian infrastructure could violate international law and further destabilize the region, risking a broader war.

stories.headline: Amazon Imposes 3.5% Surcharge on Sellers as Rising Oil Prices Drive Up Shipping Costs
stories.summary:
Amazon announced it will impose a 3.5% surcharge on sellers using its fulfillment services, citing surging oil prices driven by the ongoing U.S.-Iran conflict as the primary factor pushing up shipping and logistics costs across the American economy. The move signals a broader pass-through of wartime energy disruptions to consumers and small businesses that rely on Amazon's vast retail and fulfillment network.

The surcharge adds to mounting economic pressures already facing American businesses and households, with fuel prices rising sharply since the Strait of Hormuz was blocked. Despite soaring jet fuel prices, air fares have not yet risen proportionally, though analysts say that gap is expected to close in coming weeks as airline contracts roll over.

Economists and business groups warn that the compounding effect of higher energy costs, supply chain disruptions, and new surcharges from major logistics providers could accelerate inflation and dampen consumer spending. The Iran war's economic toll is increasingly being described as a de facto tax on American commerce, affecting everything from grocery distribution to manufacturing supply chains.

Proponents of the administration's military posture argue that short-term economic pain is an acceptable cost to achieve strategic goals, including keeping Iran from developing nuclear capabilities and reopening a critical global waterway. Critics counter that the costs are falling disproportionately on small businesses and lower-income consumers who have the least ability to absorb them.

stories.headline: Iran-U.S. War Escalates: Israeli Strikes Hit Tehran Residential Areas, Iranian Missiles Strike Haifa as Ceasefire Plan Emerges
stories.summary:
The Iran-U.S. conflict intensified sharply overnight Sunday into Monday, with Israel carrying out a "wave of strikes" on targets in Tehran, including residential buildings in Baharestan County southwest of the capital, killing at least 13 people according to Iranian state media. Simultaneously, Iranian missile strikes hit residential areas in the northern Israeli city of Haifa, with two people confirmed dead and two others missing under rubble at one site. A second Haifa location was also struck, with active fires reported in what appeared to be residential neighborhoods.

On the ground in Iran, newly released satellite imagery from Airbus showed at least 28 craters along roadways in central Esfahan province, evidence of precision strikes apparently designed to destroy road infrastructure during the rescue of a downed U.S. airman. Iranian state media also reported strikes on Ahvaz, Bandar Lengeh, Karaj, and Shiraz, with at least six people killed and 17 wounded in the Bandar Lengeh and Kong area. Iran's Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps reported the death of a senior officer, identified as Majid Khademi, on Monday morning.

A significant diplomatic development emerged Monday when a source told Reuters that both Iran and the United States had received a plan to end hostilities, potentially including an immediate ceasefire that could take effect Monday and reopen the Strait of Hormuz. Behind-the-scenes diplomatic efforts have involved Oman, Pakistan, and Egypt working to keep communications open between Washington and Tehran. President Trump had set a new deadline of Tuesday at 8:00 p.m. Eastern Time for Iran to reopen the strait, while issuing renewed threats to strike power plants and other infrastructure.

Iranian officials have remained publicly defiant, stating the strait will remain closed until Iran receives compensation for war damages. The escalating civilian casualties on both sides — in Tehran, Haifa, and other urban areas — have drawn renewed international concern about the humanitarian toll of the conflict, even as mediators press both governments to accept a ceasefire framework.

stories.headline: Iran and U.S. Receive Ceasefire Proposal That Could Reopen Strait of Hormuz
stories.summary:
A source with knowledge of the proposals told Reuters on Monday that both Iran and the United States have received a plan to end hostilities that could come into effect immediately and reopen the Strait of Hormuz, the critical waterway whose closure has sent oil prices sharply higher and disrupted global shipping. The plan represents the most concrete diplomatic framework to emerge since the conflict began, though neither government has publicly confirmed receipt or acceptance of the proposal.

Mediation efforts have been conducted through multiple channels, with Oman, Pakistan, and Egypt all playing roles in keeping lines of communication open between Washington and Tehran. The timing of the proposal is significant, arriving hours before President Trump's self-imposed Tuesday evening deadline for Iran to reopen the strait, a deadline Trump backed with threats of strikes on Iranian power plants and infrastructure.

Iran has publicly maintained a defiant posture, with senior officials saying the strait will remain blocked until Tehran receives reparations for war damages. Whether the ceasefire proposal includes any financial compensation mechanism or sanctions relief for Iran has not been disclosed. Acceptance by both sides would mark a dramatic reversal of the escalatory trajectory of recent days, which has seen strikes on civilian areas in both Iran and Israel.

The Strait of Hormuz carries roughly 20 percent of the world's oil supply, and its blockade has already produced significant economic disruptions for American businesses and consumers. A ceasefire that reopens the waterway would have immediate implications for global energy markets, though the underlying disputes over Iran's nuclear program and regional influence that precipitated the conflict would likely require longer-term negotiations to resolve.

stories.headline: Trump Endorses Steve Hilton in California Governor's Race
stories.summary:
President Trump has endorsed British-born media personality and former Fox News host Steve Hilton in the 2026 California governor's race, according to Politico. The endorsement injects the president directly into a competitive Republican primary in a state Democrats have dominated for decades, and comes despite warnings from some Republicans who argued the party's best electoral prospects depended on Trump staying out of the contest.

Hilton, who is a naturalized U.S. citizen and has lived in California, built a following as a commentator aligned with Trump's brand of populist conservatism. Supporters of the endorsement argue Trump's backing could energize the Republican base and raise Hilton's national profile and fundraising capacity in an expensive statewide race. Critics within the party contend that a Trump-endorsed candidate may struggle to win over the independent and moderate voters needed to mount a viable general election challenge in California.

The California governor's race is one of the most closely watched state contests of the 2026 election cycle, with Democrats defending incumbency in the nation's most populous state amid economic anxieties and ongoing debates over housing, crime, and immigration policy. A Republican victory, while historically unlikely in California, would represent a major political realignment.

Trump's decision to weigh in follows a broader pattern of the president intervening in Republican primaries across the country, a strategy that has produced mixed results in past cycles, with some Trump-backed candidates winning competitive races and others losing winnable seats.

stories.headline: FDA Approves New GLP-1 Drug From Eli Lilly as Demand for Weight-Loss Medications Surges
stories.summary:
The U.S. Food and Drug Administration has approved a new GLP-1 medication developed by Eli Lilly, expanding the field of glucagon-like peptide-1 receptor agonists that have reshaped treatment of obesity and type 2 diabetes. The approval adds to a growing class of drugs that includes semaglutide, sold under the brand names Ozempic and Wegovy by Novo Nordisk.

GLP-1 medications have drawn intense scientific and public interest in recent years, with studies suggesting benefits extending well beyond blood sugar control and weight loss. A large study cited this week found that GLP-1 users, including those taking semaglutide, experienced significant reductions in depression, anxiety, and psychiatric-related hospital visits. Separately, researchers are examining how genetics may influence whether such drugs also affect brain health and Alzheimer's disease risk.

The expanding approval landscape reflects both strong clinical demand and the commercial stakes involved, as Eli Lilly competes directly with Novo Nordisk in one of the pharmaceutical industry's fastest-growing markets. Analysts have projected that the GLP-1 drug category could reach hundreds of billions of dollars in annual global sales within the next decade.

A parallel study published this week also questioned whether body mass index — the metric most commonly used to determine obesity treatment eligibility — accurately reflects health risk for a significant portion of patients, a finding that could affect how insurers and physicians determine who qualifies for these medications.

stories.headline: U.S.-Iran War 'Tax' Begins Hitting American Businesses and Consumers as Economic Disruptions Widen
stories.summary:
The ongoing military conflict between the United States and Iran is generating measurable economic pain for American businesses and consumers, with analysts describing the accumulating costs as a de facto "war tax" on the broader economy. Supply chain disruptions, higher energy prices, and elevated shipping costs are among the pressures being tracked by economists and financial markets.

Bloomberg reported that oil prices have moved sharply higher amid the conflict's escalation, compounding inflationary pressures that were already a concern heading into 2026. Marketplace economists warned that inflation could overtake wage growth, which would reduce American households' capacity for discretionary spending. Iran's near-total internet blackout, in place since January, has also disrupted jobs and businesses inside Iran with ripple effects on regional trade.

International financial institutions are watching closely as developing nations weigh the reliability of U.S. economic leadership against China's steadier, if mercantilist, posture. Analysts note that rising adoption of Chinese technology across the developing world partly reflects uncertainty about U.S. policy during the conflict period.

Financial markets have been rattled by what analysts describe as mixed messaging from the White House. President Trump told Fox News that Iran was negotiating and that a deal was possible by Monday, even as military operations and harsh rhetoric continued, contributing to volatility that market participants say has made strategic planning difficult for businesses with international exposure.

stories.headline: White House Proposes $5.6 Billion Cut to NASA Budget for 2027 as Part of Sweeping Spending Overhaul
stories.summary:
The Trump administration has proposed cutting NASA's 2027 budget by $5.6 billion, including a $3.4 billion reduction to the space agency's science division — a 23 percent cut — as part of its broader fiscal blueprint that pairs major increases in defense spending with deep reductions to domestic and discretionary programs. The proposal was released Friday alongside the administration's request for $1.5 trillion in Pentagon funding, a roughly 42 percent increase over current levels.

The science cuts would represent a significant rollback of NASA's research programs at a time when the agency is pursuing ambitious planetary science, climate observation, and astrophysics missions. NASA's new administrator has outlined plans for an array of new initiatives, though critics say those ambitions are difficult to reconcile with the proposed funding reductions. The Artemis II lunar mission, which would send astronauts around the moon, is among the programs whose future funding is under scrutiny.

Supporters of the budget restructuring argue that fiscal discipline and a focus on defense priorities are necessary given the current geopolitical environment, including the ongoing conflict with Iran. Opponents, including some members of Congress, contend that cutting science and research infrastructure will harm U.S. competitiveness and set back decades of investment in space exploration and climate monitoring.

The proposal still requires congressional approval and is expected to face resistance from both parties, particularly members representing states and districts with large NASA facilities and contractor workforces. Budget negotiations are expected to extend well into the fall.

stories.headline: Colombia Honors 69 Security Personnel Killed in Military Plane Crash in Solemn Bogota Ceremony
stories.summary:
Colombia held a solemn state ceremony in Bogota to honor the 69 members of the nation's security forces who perished in a military plane crash. The disaster is one of the deadliest aviation accidents in the country's military history and has prompted a period of national mourning.

Officials attended the ceremony to pay tribute to those killed, though full details of the crash's cause and location have not been widely specified in initial reports. Military aviation accidents in Colombia have historically occurred in the challenging terrain of the Andes mountain range and in regions where the armed forces conduct operations against illegal armed groups.

The crash comes as Colombia's government has been navigating complex security challenges, including ongoing negotiations and operations against drug trafficking organizations and dissident guerrilla factions. The loss of 69 security force members in a single incident represents a significant blow to military capacity and morale.

Colombian President Gustavo Petro and senior defense officials were expected to lead the commemorations. No additional details on the investigation into the crash's cause were immediately available from the initial reports.
